stem: The system \( ([[a]])x + ([[b]])y = [[c1]] \) and \( kx + ([[d]])y = [[c2]] \) fails to have a unique solution for exactly one value of \( k \). What is that value of \( k \)?
solution: A 2x2 system has a unique solution exactly when its coefficient determinant is nonzero. Here the determinant is \( ([[a]])([[d]]) - ([[b]])k \), which is zero when \( k = \dfrac{([[a]])([[d]])}{[[b]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( ad \); you must still divide by \( b \), the coefficient of \( k \), to solve the determinant equation.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This adds two coefficients instead of solving \( ad - bk = 0 \).
This is off by one from the value that makes the determinant zero.
This is off by one from the value that makes the determinant zero.
This is off by two from the value that makes the determinant zero.

stem: The lines \( ([[a1]])x + ([[b1]])y = [[c1]] \) and \( ([[a2]])x + ([[b2]])y = k \) have PROPORTIONAL left-hand sides (one is a multiple of the other), so for most values of \( k \) they are parallel with no common point. For what value of \( k \) do the two equations instead describe the SAME line (infinitely many common solutions)?
solution: Since the left-hand side of the second equation is \( [[t]] \) times the first, the two equations describe the same line exactly when the right-hand sides scale the same way: \( k = [[t]]([[c1]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to scale the constant term by the same factor [[t]] as the coefficients.
This adds the scale factor instead of multiplying by it.
This divides by the scale factor instead of multiplying by it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: The lines \( ([[a1]])x + ([[b1]])y = [[c1]] \) and \( ([[a2]])x + ([[b2]])y = [[c2]] \) intersect at a single point. For what value of \( k \) does the third line \( ([[a3]])x + ([[b3]])y = k \) also pass through that same point (i.e. all three lines are concurrent)?
solution: First find the common point of the first two lines by Cramer's rule: \( x_0 = \dfrac{c_1 b_2 - c_2 b_1}{a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1} \), \( y_0 = \dfrac{a_1 c_2 - a_2 c_1}{a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1} \). The third line passes through \( (x_0,y_0) \) exactly when \( k = ([[a3]])x_0 + ([[b3]])y_0 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This just adds the two given constants instead of substituting the actual intersection point.
This plugs \( c_1, c_2 \) directly in for \( x_0, y_0 \) instead of solving for the true intersection.
This mixes up coefficients from the wrong equations instead of using the solved intersection point.
This is off by one from the correct value of \( k \).
This is off by one from the correct value of \( k \).
This is off by two from the correct value of \( k \).
